questions.question: Explain the difference between a Vertex Buffer Object (VBO) and an Index Buffer Object (IBO). How do they work together in rendering geometry?
keyPoints: VBO stores raw vertex data (positions, normals, UVs, colors) in GPU memory
IBO stores indices that reference vertices in the VBO
IBO enables vertex reuse - same vertex can be shared by multiple triangles
Together they form indexed rendering - the GPU looks up vertices by their index
This reduces memory compared to non-indexed rendering where each triangle has unique vertices

questions.question: Describe the evolution of graphics APIs from fixed-function pipeline to programmable pipeline. What are the advantages of the programmable pipeline?
keyPoints: Fixed-function pipeline had hardcoded transformation and lighting stages
Programmable pipeline allows custom shader programs for any pipeline stage
Advantages: unlimited visual effects, realistic rendering, GPU parallelism, hardware abstraction
Shader languages (GLSL, HLSL) allow developers to control GPU behavior
Modern APIs (Vulkan, DirectX 12) provide even lower-level control

questions.question: What is the role of the Vertex Shader in the graphics pipeline? Explain its input and output data.
keyPoints: Vertex shader processes each vertex individually before rasterization
Inputs: vertex attributes (position, normal, UV), uniforms (matrices, time)
Outputs: transformed position (to clip space), varyings to fragment shader (colors, UVs, normals)
Performs model-view-projection transformation, lighting calculations, skinning
Cannot create or destroy vertices - 1:1 input-output ratio

questions.question: Explain the state machine model used by OpenGL. How does it affect how graphics programs are written?
keyPoints: OpenGL operates as a state machine - current state affects all subsequent operations
State includes: current color, matrix mode, blending, depth testing, texture bindings, etc.
State persists until explicitly changed - programs must manage state carefully
State changes can be expensive - batching similar draw calls improves performance
Modern OpenGL uses objects (VAOs, FBOs) to encapsulate state

questions.question: What are uniforms in shader programming? How are they used to pass data from CPU to GPU?
keyPoints: Uniforms are global variables in shaders that remain constant across all vertices/fragments in a draw call
Set by the CPU application before issuing draw calls using API functions like glUniformMatrix4fv()
Used for: transformation matrices, light positions, material properties, time values
Unlike attributes (per-vertex), uniforms are constant for the entire draw operation
Stored in uniform storage on GPU, accessible by all shader stages

questions.question: Differentiate between immediate mode and retained mode rendering. Give examples of APIs or approaches that use each.
keyPoints: Immediate mode: geometry sent directly each frame, no persistent storage (legacy OpenGL glBegin/glEnd, old DirectX immediate mode)
Retained mode: scene graph maintained in memory, system handles rendering automatically (HTML5 Canvas 2D, scene graph libraries like OpenSceneGraph)
Immediate mode: simpler but less efficient, harder to optimize
Retained mode: better for complex scenes, automatic culling and optimization
Modern graphics APIs are neither purely immediate nor retained - they use retained data (VBOs) with immediate draw calls

questions.question: Explain the purpose and workflow of creating a shader program in OpenGL.
keyPoints: Step 1: Create shader objects with glCreateShader() for each shader type
Step 2: Load shader source code and compile with glShaderSource() and glCompileShader()
Step 3: Check compilation status with glGetShaderiv() for errors
Step 4: Create program object with glCreateProgram()
Step 5: Attach compiled shaders with glAttachShader()
Step 6: Link program with glLinkProgram() - combines shaders into executable
Step 7: Use program with glUseProgram() before draw calls
Step 8: Clean up shader objects with glDeleteShader() after linking

questions.question: Compare OpenGL, DirectX, and Vulkan as graphics programmer interfaces. Discuss their design philosophies, portability, performance characteristics, and use cases.
keyPoints: OpenGL: Cross-platform, state-machine model, intermediate abstraction, good for cross-platform development
DirectX: Windows/Xbox exclusive, COM-based object-oriented design, tight hardware optimization, dominant in gaming
Vulkan: Low-overhead explicit control, cross-platform, requires manual resource management, best for performance-critical applications
OpenGL has higher-level abstraction hiding complexity; Vulkan exposes it for control
Performance: Vulkan offers most control but steep learning curve; OpenGL simpler but some overhead
Use cases: OpenGL for cross-platform apps, DirectX for Windows games, Vulkan for high-performance/gpu-intensive apps

questions.question: Describe the complete graphics rendering pipeline from vertex data input to final pixel output. Explain each major stage and how data flows through it.
keyPoints: Input Assembly: Reads vertices from VBOs/IBOs, assembles into primitives (points, lines, triangles)
Vertex Shader: Transforms vertices, applies MVP matrix, calculates per-vertex attributes
Tessellation (optional): Subdivides primitives for smoother surfaces
Geometry Shader (optional): Can create/modify primitives
Rasterization: Converts primitives to fragments, interpolates per-vertex attributes to per-fragment
Fragment Shader: Calculates final fragment color using interpolated values, textures, lighting
Per-Fragment Operations: Depth test, stencil test, blending with framebuffer
Output Merger: Writes final color/depth to framebuffer
Each stage is programmable or fixed-function depending on API version

questions.question: Explain what Framebuffer Objects (FBOs) are and describe at least three practical applications where they are essential in computer graphics.
keyPoints: FBO is a collection of renderable textures (color, depth, stencil) serving as rendering destination instead of screen
Created with glGenFramebuffers(), bound with glBindFramebuffer()
Applications: 1) Post-processing: render scene to texture, apply effects (blur, bloom) in second pass
2) Shadow mapping: render depth from light's perspective to texture for shadow calculations
3) Render-to-texture: create reflections by rendering scene from reflected camera view
4) Multi-pass rendering: complex effects requiring intermediate results
5) Off-screen rendering: GUI rendering, screenshot capture, pixel manipulation
FBOs enable flexible rendering pipelines beyond single-pass screen output
